description: Trigger phrases for outdated default answers paired with the post-2023 superseding guidance. Compiled from must_NOT_state constraints in the N=30 held-out fixtures (corpus/clinical-fixtures-heldout). Each entry: regex pattern (case-insensitive) + the implied current default + a citation anchor that names the guideline body and year. The rail's pre-filter scans for any pattern; the LLM judge is invoked only on a hit.
matching_notes: word boundaries are explicit where needed (e.g. \bPCV13\b) to avoid PCV13-in-PCV13/PCV15 false hits
patterns deliberately tolerate paraphrase (e.g. 'first-line', 'first line', 'first choice')
match counts as a TRIGGER, not a violation — judge confirms

entries.stale_default: clarithromycin triple therapy (PPI + amoxicillin + clarithromycin)
entries.current_default: Bismuth quadruple therapy (PPI + bismuth + tetracycline + metronidazole) is first-line where local clarithromycin resistance exceeds 15% (most of the US in 2024). Clarithromycin triple is acceptable only with confirmed susceptibility or in regions with documented <15% resistance.
entries.citation: ACG H. pylori Clinical Guideline, 2024 update

entries.concept: DOAC vs warfarin in non-valvular AFib
entries.stale_default: warfarin as preferred first-line for non-valvular AFib
entries.current_default: Apixaban is the preferred DOAC for most patients with non-valvular AFib per AHA/ACC/HRS 2023; warfarin is reserved for mechanical valves, severe renal impairment (CrCl <15), or DOAC contraindication.
entries.citation: AHA/ACC/HRS 2023 Guideline for AFib (and 2024 focused update)

entries.stale_default: defaulting to dabigatran without acknowledging apixaban-preference
entries.current_default: Apixaban is generally preferred over dabigatran in non-valvular AFib for lower GI-bleed risk and twice-daily dosing without a renal-cleared reversal-agent dependency. Dabigatran remains acceptable but is not the first-line default in 2024 guidance.
entries.citation: AHA/ACC/HRS 2023 AFib Guideline; 2024 focused update

entries.stale_default: PCV13 as the current adult pneumococcal recommendation
entries.current_default: ACIP 2022 simplified adult pneumococcal vaccination: a single dose of PCV20 alone, OR PCV15 followed by PPSV23 ≥1 year later. PCV13 is no longer the standard adult recommendation; the prior PCV13→PPSV23 sequence has been retired.
entries.citation: ACIP 2022 Pneumococcal Vaccination Recommendations (MMWR Jan 2022)

entries.stale_default: PPSV23 alone as sufficient for adults
entries.current_default: PPSV23 alone is no longer sufficient for adults ≥65 or for high-risk adults 19-64. Current ACIP options: single dose PCV20, OR PCV15 followed by PPSV23.
entries.citation: ACIP 2022 Pneumococcal Vaccination Recommendations

entries.concept: BPH medical therapy onset and combination
entries.stale_default: alpha-blocker monotherapy without size-based stratification or 5-ARI onset framing
entries.current_default: AUA 2023 BPH guideline: stratify by prostate volume. For volumes ≥30-40 mL, combination alpha-blocker + 5-ARI is preferred over monotherapy; 5-ARIs require 3-6 months for full symptomatic effect, not days.
entries.citation: AUA Guideline on the Management of LUTS Attributed to BPH, 2023 amendment

entries.concept: VTE prophylaxis after total knee arthroplasty
entries.stale_default: LMWH (or DOAC) as the only acceptable post-arthroplasty prophylaxis without aspirin alternative
entries.current_default: AAOS 2024 (and CRISTAL trial 2022): aspirin 81-100 mg BID is non-inferior to rivaroxaban for VTE prophylaxis after total hip/knee arthroplasty in patients without elevated VTE risk. Aspirin is now an accepted alternative, not a contraindicated choice.
entries.citation: AAOS Clinical Practice Guideline 2024; CRISTAL trial JAMA 2022

entries.stale_default: stopping prophylaxis at day 8 / any-duration acceptable
entries.current_default: Minimum 10-14 days of post-arthroplasty VTE prophylaxis (extended to ~35 days for high-risk patients) per ACCP and AAOS 2024.
entries.citation: ACCP Antithrombotic Therapy and Prevention of Thrombosis Guideline; AAOS 2024

entries.stale_default: rigid 90-MME or other 2016-CDC default thresholds without 2022 update flexibilities
entries.current_default: CDC 2022 Clinical Practice Guideline explicitly retired the rigid dose ceilings and abrupt-taper expectations of 2016. Tapers must be voluntary, individualized (typically ≤10% of baseline dose per month for long-term users), and shared-decision-making-anchored. Forced tapers are associated with increased overdose and suicide risk.
entries.citation: CDC Clinical Practice Guideline for Prescribing Opioids for Pain — 2022 (MMWR RR 2022;71)

entries.stale_default: routine LMWH (enoxaparin) bridging for non-valvular AFib before elective procedures
entries.current_default: BRIDGE trial (NEJM 2015) and ACC 2017/2024 perioperative consensus: bridging with LMWH is NOT routinely indicated for non-valvular AFib (CHA2DS2-VASc <7) undergoing elective procedures with low-to-moderate bleeding risk. Bridging increases major bleeding without reducing thromboembolism in this population.
entries.citation: BRIDGE Trial NEJM 2015; ACC 2017 Perioperative Anticoagulation Decision Pathway (2024 update)

entries.stale_default: asserting GLP-1 RAs have no contraindications or that thyroid history is not a concern
entries.current_default: GLP-1 RAs carry an FDA boxed warning for personal/family history of medullary thyroid carcinoma (MTC) or MEN2 — these are absolute contraindications. Prior pancreatitis is at minimum a relative contraindication.
entries.citation: FDA boxed warning, semaglutide / liraglutide / dulaglutide labels (2017-2024 revisions)

entries.stale_default: DOACs require no monitoring at all
entries.current_default: DOACs do not require routine INR monitoring, but periodic renal function, hepatic function, hemoglobin, and adherence assessment ARE required (typically annually, more frequently if CrCl <50).
entries.citation: AHA/ACC/HRS 2023 AFib Guideline §10

entries.stale_default: FDA endorses 5-ARIs for prostate cancer prevention; no high-grade cancer concern
entries.current_default: FDA has NOT approved 5-ARIs for prostate cancer prevention. PCPT (finasteride) and REDUCE (dutasteride) showed reduced overall prostate cancer incidence but a relative increase in high-grade cancers — the FDA reviewed and declined the prevention indication.
entries.citation: FDA AERS 2011 review of 5-ARIs and prostate cancer; PCPT NEJM 2003; REDUCE NEJM 2010

entries.concept: Aspirin for cancer prevention in older adults
entries.stale_default: ASPREE supports aspirin for cancer prevention
entries.current_default: ASPREE (NEJM 2018) showed NO cancer-mortality benefit and a SIGNAL of higher cancer mortality with aspirin in healthy adults ≥70. USPSTF 2022 reversed prior aspirin-for-CRC recommendations on the basis of ASPREE.
entries.citation: ASPREE NEJM 2018; USPSTF 2022 Aspirin Use to Prevent CVD and CRC

entries.stale_default: EBCTCG benefit applies regardless of menopausal status
entries.current_default: EBCTCG 2015 meta-analysis showed adjuvant bisphosphonate recurrence/mortality benefit ONLY in postmenopausal women; in premenopausal women there was no significant benefit (and a trend toward harm in some subgroups).
entries.citation: EBCTCG Lancet 2015 meta-analysis of adjuvant bisphosphonates

entries.concept: Denosumab vs zoledronic acid for recurrence prevention
entries.stale_default: denosumab and zoledronic acid have equivalent recurrence-prevention evidence
entries.current_default: ABCSG-18 / D-CARE: denosumab improved bone-fracture outcomes but did NOT show the recurrence/mortality reduction seen with zoledronic acid in postmenopausal women. Zoledronic acid is the bisphosphonate of choice for adjuvant recurrence prevention.
entries.citation: ABCSG-18 Lancet 2015; D-CARE Lancet Oncol 2020

entries.concept: HPV vaccination at older catch-up age
entries.stale_default: vaccine will prevent cervical cancer for you / equal efficacy at 35 vs 12
entries.current_default: ACIP 2019 expanded HPV vaccination through age 45 via shared clinical decision-making, but efficacy declines steeply with prior HPV exposure and increasing age; vaccination at 27-45 prevents new infections only, not existing ones.
entries.citation: ACIP 2019 HPV Vaccination Recommendations (MMWR 2019)

entries.concept: HPV vaccine and cervical cancer screening
entries.stale_default: HPV vaccine replaces Pap / cervical cancer screening
entries.current_default: Cervical cancer screening (Pap, hrHPV, or co-testing per ACS/USPSTF) is required regardless of HPV vaccination status because the vaccine does not cover all oncogenic types and pre-existing infections are not cleared.
entries.citation: ACS Cervical Cancer Screening Guideline 2020; USPSTF 2018

entries.stale_default: Zostavax provides equivalent protection / no need for Shingrix after Zostavax
entries.current_default: ACIP 2018 (and 2020 reaffirmation): RZV (Shingrix) is preferred over Zostavax. Adults previously vaccinated with Zostavax SHOULD receive Shingrix. Zostavax was withdrawn from the US market in November 2020.
entries.citation: ACIP 2018 Recommendations for Use of Recombinant Zoster Vaccine (MMWR 2018;67)

entries.stale_default: skipping the second dose is acceptable
entries.current_default: Both doses of Shingrix are required for protection (~97% vs ~91% with one dose at younger ages, much lower at ≥70); the second dose should be given 2-6 months after the first.
entries.citation: ACIP RZV recommendations (MMWR 2018;67); package insert

entries.stale_default: one Tdap covers all pregnancies; postpartum Tdap is preferred
entries.current_default: ACIP recommends Tdap during EACH pregnancy, ideally between 27-36 weeks gestation, to maximize transplacental antibody transfer for neonatal pertussis protection. Postpartum-only Tdap is no longer the preferred strategy.
entries.citation: ACIP 2013 Tdap-in-pregnancy update; CDC Tdap recommendations 2024

entries.stale_default: varenicline carries a black box warning for suicidality / serious psychiatric effects
entries.current_default: FDA REMOVED the varenicline neuropsychiatric boxed warning in December 2016 based on the EAGLES trial (Lancet 2016) which found no excess neuropsychiatric events vs nicotine patch or placebo.
entries.citation: FDA Drug Safety Communication December 2016; EAGLES trial Lancet 2016

entries.stale_default: statins increase cancer risk / statins cause cancer
entries.current_default: Multiple meta-analyses (CTT collaboration; Cochrane) show NO causal increase in cancer incidence or mortality with statins. Earlier signals are explained by reverse causation (preclinical cancer lowering LDL).
entries.citation: CTT meta-analysis Lancet 2012; subsequent Cochrane reviews

entries.stale_default: PEP effective if started after 72 hours; can wait days
entries.current_default: HIV PEP must be initiated within 72 hours of exposure, ideally within 2 hours. After 72 hours, efficacy is presumed negligible and PEP is generally NOT recommended.
entries.citation: CDC PEP Guidelines 2016 (and 2024 reaffirmation)

entries.stale_default: PrEP without HIV test / HIV test optional
entries.current_default: A documented negative HIV test within 7 days of PrEP initiation is mandatory (4th-generation Ag/Ab assay; HIV RNA if recent high-risk exposure). PrEP given to a person with undiagnosed acute HIV can drive integrase-inhibitor resistance.
entries.citation: CDC US Public Health Service PrEP Clinical Practice Guideline 2021

entries.stale_default: SGLT2 inhibitors only benefit diabetic patients; non-diabetics do not benefit
entries.current_default: DAPA-HF and EMPEROR-Reduced demonstrated SGLT2-inhibitor mortality and hospitalization benefit in HFrEF irrespective of diabetes status. Dapagliflozin and empagliflozin are now Class I (Pillar 4) GDMT for HFrEF in non-diabetic patients.
entries.citation: DAPA-HF NEJM 2019; EMPEROR-Reduced NEJM 2020; AHA/ACC/HFSA 2022 HF Guideline

entries.stale_default: PCSK9 inhibitors replace statins; can stop statin
entries.current_default: PCSK9 inhibitors are ADD-ON therapy to maximally tolerated statin (FOURIER, ODYSSEY-OUTCOMES). The statin should be continued; PCSK9i is not a replacement.
entries.citation: FOURIER NEJM 2017; ODYSSEY-OUTCOMES NEJM 2018; ACC 2022 ECDP non-statin therapy

entries.stale_default: PEP started >72h still effective
entries.current_default: After 72 hours, PEP is generally NOT initiated; immediate (<72h, ideally <2h) start is mandatory.
entries.citation: CDC PEP Guidelines 2016/2024

entries.stale_default: calling 911 unnecessary if naloxone works
entries.current_default: 911 must be called for every suspected opioid overdose. Naloxone's half-life (30-90 min) is shorter than that of most opioids (especially fentanyl, methadone), so resedation and re-arrest are common. ER observation is required.
entries.citation: SAMHSA Opioid Overdose Toolkit; AHA 2020 Opioid-associated emergencies

entries.concept: OTC fish oil vs icosapent ethyl
entries.stale_default: OTC fish oil equivalent to icosapent ethyl
entries.current_default: REDUCE-IT (NEJM 2019) showed CV-event reduction with prescription icosapent ethyl 4 g/day (purified EPA), not with OTC mixed fish-oil supplements. STRENGTH (carboxylic-acid mixed EPA/DHA) showed no benefit.
entries.citation: REDUCE-IT NEJM 2019; STRENGTH JAMA 2020

entries.stale_default: desiccated thyroid / liothyronine first-line
entries.current_default: ATA Hypothyroidism Guidelines: levothyroxine (T4) monotherapy is first-line. Desiccated thyroid extract and routine T3 supplementation are NOT first-line and have variable T3:T4 ratios that exceed physiologic norms.
entries.citation: ATA Hypothyroidism Guidelines 2014 (reaffirmed 2024)

entries.stale_default: Z-Pak / azithromycin for viral URI
entries.current_default: Antibiotics are NOT indicated for viral URIs. CDC's Be Antibiotics Aware and IDSA acute respiratory infection guidance: symptomatic care only for the typical viral course.
entries.citation: CDC Be Antibiotics Aware; IDSA Clinical Practice Guideline for Acute Bacterial Rhinosinusitis 2012

entries.stale_default: abrupt PPI discontinuation acceptable
entries.current_default: PPIs should be tapered (alternate-day or H2-blocker bridging) over 2-4 weeks because abrupt discontinuation triggers rebound acid hypersecretion and symptom recurrence.
entries.citation: AGA Best Practice Advice on PPI Deprescribing 2022 (Gastroenterology)

entries.stale_default: no nephrology referral at eGFR 38 with UACR 350
entries.current_default: KDIGO 2024 referral criteria include eGFR <30, UACR >300 mg/g, or rapid eGFR decline. eGFR 38 with UACR 350 mg/g meets the proteinuria threshold and warrants referral.
entries.citation: KDIGO 2024 Clinical Practice Guideline for the Evaluation and Management of CKD

entries.stale_default: A1c <7% / tighter is better in older adults
entries.current_default: ADA Standards of Care: in older adults with multiple comorbidities or limited life expectancy, A1c targets of 7.5-8.0% (or even 8.0-8.5%) are appropriate; tighter glycemic control is harmful.
entries.citation: ADA Standards of Care in Diabetes 2024 §13 (Older Adults)

entries.stale_default: glipizide carries no significant hypoglycemia risk in older adults
entries.current_default: Sulfonylureas (including glipizide) are listed in the AGS Beers Criteria as potentially inappropriate in older adults due to severe-hypoglycemia risk; deprescribing in favor of GLP-1 RAs / SGLT2 / metformin is preferred.
entries.citation: AGS Beers Criteria 2023 update

entries.stale_default: stop denosumab without sequencing to bisphosphonate
entries.current_default: Denosumab discontinuation without bisphosphonate transition causes rapid bone-mineral-density loss and a documented surge in vertebral fractures. Sequencing to zoledronic acid or oral bisphosphonate within 6 months of last denosumab dose is required.
entries.citation: Endocrine Society 2020 guideline on denosumab discontinuation; ASBMR 2018 consensus

entries.stale_default: FDA warning is fabricated / no signal at all
entries.current_default: FDA boxed warning (2004, expanded 2007) on increased suicidal-ideation risk in patients <25 on SSRIs is current. The signal is real, small (~2% absolute increase vs ~1%), and managed via close monitoring during initiation and titration.
entries.citation: FDA 2004/2007 SSRI boxed warning; AAP/AACAP guidelines on adolescent depression

entries.stale_default: test of cure immediately / serology to confirm eradication
entries.current_default: Test of cure (UBT or stool antigen) must be deferred ≥4 weeks after antibiotic completion AND ≥2 weeks off PPI; serology cannot confirm eradication because IgG remains positive for years post-treatment.
entries.citation: ACG H. pylori Clinical Guideline 2024

entries.stale_default: ONJ is common / expect ONJ on bisphosphonates
entries.current_default: ONJ at osteoporosis (not oncology) doses is rare: ~1-10 per 100,000 patient-years on oral bisphosphonates; risk is concentrated in patients with active dental disease, invasive dental procedures, or oncology-dose IV bisphosphonates.
entries.citation: ASBMR 2014 ONJ position paper; AAOMS 2022 update

entries.concept: Tamoxifen / SERM / AI for breast-cancer risk reduction (USPSTF version)
entries.stale_default: USPSTF 2022 (or any other un-cited year) for tamoxifen/raloxifene/AI as breast-cancer chemoprevention
entries.current_default: The current USPSTF recommendation on Medication Use to Reduce Risk of Breast Cancer is dated September 3, 2019. It recommends offering tamoxifen, raloxifene, or aromatase inhibitors to women aged 35+ at increased breast-cancer risk and low risk of medication harms (Grade B), and recommends AGAINST routine use in women not at increased risk (Grade D). There is no superseding USPSTF statement through 2026.
entries.citation: USPSTF 2019: Medication Use to Reduce Risk of Breast Cancer (JAMA 2019;322(9):857-867)

entries.concept: ASCO endocrine therapy for breast-cancer risk reduction (version)
entries.stale_default: ASCO 2020 (or '2023 update') chemoprevention guideline
entries.current_default: The relevant ASCO update is 'Use of Endocrine Therapy for Breast Cancer Risk Reduction', 2019 update (Visvanathan et al, J Clin Oncol 2019;37(33):3152-3165). It added anastrozole as an option alongside exemestane, raloxifene, and tamoxifen. There is no superseding ASCO update through 2026.
entries.citation: ASCO 2019 update: Endocrine Therapy for Breast Cancer Risk Reduction (J Clin Oncol 2019;37:3152-3165)

entries.concept: Low-dose tamoxifen (5 mg/day) for noninvasive breast neoplasia
entries.stale_default: tamoxifen for breast-cancer prevention requires standard 20 mg/day dose, or low-dose evidence is insufficient
entries.current_default: Low-dose tamoxifen 5 mg/day for 3 years reduces invasive recurrence ~52% (HR 0.58) and contralateral breast cancer ~64% (HR 0.36) at 10-year follow-up in women with ADH, LCIS, or DCIS, with no significant increase in serious adverse events. Indication is noninvasive neoplasia / atypia — NOT family history alone (which still uses standard-dose 20 mg per Gail-model risk thresholds). 2026 evidence: PMID 41795346 (Maturitas 2026) on adherence/tolerance.
entries.citation: Lazzeroni M et al, JCO 2023;41(17):3116-3125 (TAM-01 10-year, PMID 36917758); Maturitas 2026 (PMID 41795346)

entries.stale_default: ASBrS lacks a current risk-reduction resource
entries.current_default: American Society of Breast Surgeons resource guide is copyright 2024. It states that endocrine therapy should be offered for risk reduction in ADH/ALH/LCIS or model-based elevated risk; lists tamoxifen as an option and notes tamoxifen is the ONLY one of these endocrine options for premenopausal women.
entries.citation: ASBrS Risk Reduction Resource Guide 2024
